titel: Stricter rules regarding deportation due to criminal offenses.
html:
The proposal includes suggestions that would allow for more decisions to deport foreign nationals who commit crimes. The proposals include:

  • Crimes will, as a general rule, be grounds for deportation when the penalty imposed is stricter than a fine.
  • The weighing of reasons for and against deportation will, in more cases, result in deportation.
  • The strict requirements for deporting foreign nationals who came to Sweden before the age of 15 will be removed.
  • Enforcement obstacles will no longer be considered by the district court when deciding whether or not to order deportation.
  • Re-entry bans will become longer and, in more cases, without a time limit.
  • Prosecutors will have a duty to request deportation when a foreign national is charged with crimes that are grounds for deportation.

The proposal also includes, among other things, stricter penalties for certain offenses under the Aliens Act.

The proposed amendments are to come into force on September 1, 2026.

Proposed Parliamentary Decision

The government's proposal: The Riksdag (Swedish Parliament) adopts the government's proposal for an amendment to the Aliens Act (2005:716). 4
tldr: The government proposes new, stricter regulations to facilitate the deportation of individuals who commit crimes in Sweden. The proposal aims to make it easier to decide on deportations and to increase the penalties for certain offenses under the immigration law.
lattlast:
The government has sent a proposal to the Parliament that makes it easier to deport foreign nationals who commit crimes. A major change is that almost all crimes that carry a penalty other than a fine could now lead to deportation. This means that it will be more difficult to remain in Sweden if one has committed a crime.

The proposal also changes the rules for individuals who came to Sweden before the age of 15. Previously, there were very strict requirements for deporting these individuals, but now the proposal suggests that these requirements be removed. Furthermore, prosecutors will now have a duty to demand deportation when a person is charged with a crime that could result in their removal from the country.

Another important decision concerns travel bans, which are prohibitions against returning to Sweden. The new rules mean that these bans will be longer and, in some cases, may apply indefinitely. The government also wants the courts to not consider obstacles to carrying out the deportation when deciding whether a person should be deported.

If the Parliament votes in favor of the proposal, the new laws will come into effect on September 1, 2026. The proposal concerns amendments to the Immigration Act, which is the law that governs the rules for who is allowed to reside in Sweden.
